metadata.source: WHO, CDC, AHA/ACC, GINA, ADA, APA, IDSA, ACG

data.definition: High blood pressure that does not have a known secondary cause. It is the leading risk factor for cardiovascular disease worldwide. Diagnosis requires at least two readings on separate occasions showing BP >= 130/80 mmHg (Stage 1) or >= 140/90 mmHg (Stage 2) according to AHA/ACC guidelines.
data.firstLineTreatment: Lifestyle modifications (DASH diet, sodium reduction <1500mg/day, regular aerobic activity 150 min/week, weight loss, and moderation of alcohol). Pharmacological therapy for Stage 1 (if ASCVD risk >=10%) or Stage 2: Thiazide diuretics (Chlorthalidone preferred), ACE inhibitors (Lisinopril), Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers (Losartan), or Calcium Channel Blockers (Amlodipine).
data.alternatives: Beta-blockers (not first-line unless comorbid heart failure or CAD), Alpha-blockers (Doxazosin), Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (Spironolactone for resistant HTN), and Direct vasodilators (Hydralazine).
data.redFlags: Hypertensive Urgency/Emergency: BP >180/120 mmHg. Seek immediate care if accompanied by chest pain, shortness of breath, severe headache, confusion, blurred vision, or signs of stroke (facial drooping, arm weakness, speech difficulty). Refer to cardiology if secondary causes suspected or resistant to 3+ meds.
data.followUp: Re-evaluate 1 month after starting or adjusting medication. Once blood pressure goal is achieved, follow-up every 3 to 6 months depending on comorbidities and stability.

data.definition: A chronic metabolic disorder characterized by hyperglycemia due to progressive insulin secretory defect on the background of insulin resistance. Diagnosis is based on A1C >= 6.5%, Fasting Plasma Glucose >= 126 mg/dL, or Random Glucose >= 200 mg/dL with symptoms.
data.firstLineTreatment: Metformin is the preferred initial pharmacological agent. Lifestyle management is foundational, including 150 minutes of moderate-to-vigorous aerobic activity per week, resistance training 2-3 times/week, and individualized medical nutrition therapy focused on carbohydrate quality and portion control.
data.alternatives: SGLT2 inhibitors (Empagliflozin) especially if heart failure or CKD; GLP-1 receptor agonists (Semaglutide) if ASCVD or weight loss is a priority; DPP-4 inhibitors; Pioglitazone; Sulfonylureas; and Insulin therapy for severe hyperglycemia or failure of oral agents.
data.redFlags: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) or Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State (HHS): extreme thirst, frequent urination, confusion, abdominal pain, fruity-smelling breath. Refer for non-healing foot ulcers, sudden vision loss (retinopathy), or rapid decline in kidney function (nephropathy).
data.followUp: Assess A1C every 3 months if not at goal or therapy changed; every 6 months if stable and meeting goals. Annual screening for microvascular complications: monofilament foot exam, dilated eye exam, and urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR).

data.definition: A heterogeneous disease characterized by chronic airway inflammation. It is defined by the history of respiratory symptoms such as wheeze, shortness of breath, chest tightness, and cough that vary over time and in intensity, together with variable expiratory airflow limitation.
data.firstLineTreatment: GINA 2024 recommends low-dose Inhaled Corticosteroid (ICS)-formoterol as the preferred reliever for all steps and as maintenance/reliever therapy (MART) for Steps 3-5. Alternatively, use SABA as a reliever only if paired with ICS whenever taken (Step 1-2). Daily maintenance low-dose ICS (Fluticasone) is essential to prevent exacerbations.
data.alternatives: Leukotriene receptor antagonists (Montelukast), Long-acting muscarinic antagonists (LAMA - Tiotropium), and biologic therapies (Omalizumab, Mepolizumab) for severe, refractory cases. Bronchial thermoplasty in selected patients.
data.redFlags: Acute Exacerbation: Silent chest (no air movement), cyanosis, inability to speak in full sentences, accessory muscle use, or peak flow <50% of personal best. Refer to a specialist if diagnosis is uncertain, symptoms remain uncontrolled on Step 4 therapy, or history of frequent oral steroid use.
data.followUp: Review 1–3 months after starting treatment, then every 3–12 months. After an exacerbation, follow-up within 1 week. Monitor lung function (spirometry) at least every 1–2 years, or more frequently in children and high-risk adults.

data.definition: A mental health disorder characterized by at least two weeks of pervasive low mood, low self-esteem, and loss of interest or pleasure in normally enjoyable activities. It significantly impairs social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning.
data.firstLineTreatment: Evidence-based psychotherapy (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), Interpersonal Psychotherapy (IPT)) and/or pharmacotherapy with Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs - Sertraline, Escitalopram) or Serotonin-Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitors (SNRIs - Venlafaxine). Combination therapy is more effective for moderate to severe cases.
data.alternatives: Atypical antidepressants (Bupropion, Mirtazapine), Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs), MAOIs (rarely used), and adjunctive atypical antipsychotics. For treatment-resistant depression: Ketamine/Esketamine, Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), or Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS).
data.redFlags: Suicidal ideation, plan, or intent; homicidal ideation; psychosis (hallucinations or delusions); severe self-neglect; or inability to maintain safety. Immediate psychiatric referral or emergency room visit required. Refer to a psychiatrist if refractory to two trials of antidepressants.
data.followUp: Weekly to bi-weekly monitoring during the first 4-8 weeks of treatment to assess safety, side effects, and early response. Once stable, follow up every 1-3 months. Continue medication for 6-9 months after full remission of a first episode to prevent relapse.

data.definition: Characterized by excessive, persistent, and unrealistic worry about everyday things. Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) involves worry for more days than not for at least 6 months, often accompanied by physical symptoms like muscle tension and sleep disturbance.
data.firstLineTreatment: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is the most effective psychotherapy. Pharmacotherapy includes SSRIs (Paroxetine, Sertraline) or SNRIs (Duloxetine, Venlafaxine) as first-line long-term treatments. Mindfulness-based stress reduction and regular physical exercise are strongly recommended.
data.alternatives: Pregabalin, Buspirone, and Tricyclic antidepressants. Benzodiazepines (Alprazolam, Lorazepam) may be used for short-term relief (2-4 weeks) or acute panic but are avoided long-term due to risks of dependence and cognitive impairment.
data.redFlags: Symptoms interfering with the ability to work, go to school, or maintain relationships. Co-occurring substance abuse used to self-medicate. Severe panic attacks mimicking cardiac events. Refer for specialized care if symptoms are severe, comorbid with personality disorders, or non-responsive to primary care interventions.
data.followUp: Every 2 weeks until clinical response is noted, then monthly. Evaluation of GAD-7 scores at each visit to objectively measure progress. Maintenance treatment should typically continue for at least 12 months after remission.

data.definition: Abnormally elevated levels of lipids (fats) in the blood, including cholesterol and triglycerides. It is a major contributor to atherosclerosis and increased 10-year risk of heart attack and stroke.
data.firstLineTreatment: Heart-healthy lifestyle (Mediterranean or DASH-style diet, weight management, cessation of tobacco). Statin therapy (Atorvastatin, Rosuvastatin) is first-line, with intensity (moderate vs. high) determined by 10-year ASCVD risk score and presence of clinical ASCVD or diabetes.
data.alternatives: Ezetimibe as an add-on if goals not met. PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab) for very high risk. For high triglycerides (>500 mg/dL): Fibrates (Fenofibrate) or high-dose Omega-3 fatty acids (Vascepa) to prevent pancreatitis.
data.redFlags: Clinical signs of severe hyperlipidemia: Xanthomas (fatty deposits under skin/tendons), Xanthelasma (eyelid deposits), or corneal arcus in patients <45 years. Severe muscle pain or dark urine while on statins (rhabdomyolysis risk). LDL-C >= 190 mg/dL (suggests Familial Hypercholesterolemia).
data.followUp: Fast lipid panel 4 to 12 weeks after starting or adjusting statin therapy to assess adherence and response. Once stable, follow-up every 3 to 12 months. Monitor liver enzymes (ALT) if symptoms of hepatotoxicity occur.

data.definition: Acute viral infection of the upper respiratory tract (nose, sinuses, pharynx, or larynx). Most common cause is rhinovirus. Typically self-limiting with symptoms lasting 7-10 days.
data.firstLineTreatment: Supportive care is the mainstay of treatment: adequate hydration, rest, and humidified air. Over-the-counter (OTC) analgesics (Acetaminophen or Ibuprofen) for fever and pain. Honey for cough in children >1 year. Antibiotics are NOT indicated for viral URIs.
data.alternatives: Intranasal saline rinses, oral decongestants (Pseudoephedrine) for short-term nasal relief in adults, and topical decongestants (Oxymetazoline - max 3 days). Zinc or Vitamin C may modestly reduce duration if started within 24 hours of onset.
data.redFlags: Shortness of breath, wheezing, high or prolonged fever (>3-5 days), inability to tolerate fluids (dehydration), or worsening of symptoms after initial improvement (suggests secondary bacterial infection like pneumonia or sinusitis). Seek immediate care for stridor or respiratory distress.
data.followUp: Usually none required as symptoms resolve within 2 weeks. Advise the patient to return if symptoms persist beyond 10-14 days or if red flags develop. No routine diagnostic testing is necessary for uncomplicated cases.

data.definition: Infection in any part of the urinary system, most commonly the bladder (cystitis). Characterized by dysuria (painful urination), frequency, and urgency. E. coli is the most common pathogen.
data.firstLineTreatment: Nitrofurantoin (100mg BID for 5 days), Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (one DS tablet BID for 3 days), or Fosfomycin (3g single dose). Phenazopyridine may be used for up to 2 days for symptomatic relief of dysuria.
data.alternatives: Pivmecillinam (if available), Cephalexin, or Amoxicillin-clavulanate. Fluoroquinolones (Ciprofloxacin) should be reserved for cases where other options are not appropriate due to resistance concerns and potential side effects.
data.redFlags: Systemic symptoms suggesting Pyelonephritis: flank pain, high fever, chills, nausea/vomiting. UTI in pregnancy (risk of preterm birth). UTI in men (always considered complicated). Recurrent UTIs (>=3/year). Hematuria that persists after infection clears.
data.followUp: Routine follow-up or post-treatment urine culture is NOT recommended for uncomplicated cystitis in non-pregnant women if symptoms resolve. If symptoms do not improve within 48-72 hours, culture and sensitivity should be obtained.

data.definition: A condition which develops when the reflux of stomach contents causes troublesome symptoms (heartburn, regurgitation) and/or complications (esophagitis). Primarily caused by transient lower esophageal sphincter (LES) relaxations.
data.firstLineTreatment: Weight loss (if BMI >25), elevation of the head of the bed (foam wedges), and avoidance of late-night meals (within 3 hours of sleep). Pharmacological therapy: an 8-week course of daily Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPIs - Omeprazole, Pantoprazole) taken 30-60 minutes before breakfast.
data.alternatives: Histamine-2 receptor antagonists (H2RAs - Famotidine) for milder symptoms or as 'on-demand' therapy. Antacids (Tums) for immediate, short-term relief. Baclofen for refractory regurgitation. Surgical options like Nissen fundoplication for selected patients.
data.redFlags: Alarm symptoms: Dysphagia (difficulty swallowing), odynophagia (painful swallowing), unexplained weight loss, persistent vomiting, gastrointestinal bleeding (melena or hematemesis), or new-onset symptoms in patients >60 years. Long-standing GERD requires screening for Barrett's esophagus.
data.followUp: Re-assess after 8-week PPI course. If symptoms are resolved, attempt to taper or discontinue the medication. If symptoms recur or are refractory, refer to gastroenterology for endoscopy (EGD) or pH monitoring.

data.definition: A primary headache disorder characterized by recurrent attacks of moderate-to-severe throbbing and pulsating pain, often unilateral, and associated with nausea, vomiting, and sensitivity to light (photophobia) and sound (phonophobia). May occur with or without aura.
data.firstLineTreatment: Abortive: Triptans (Sumatriptan, Rizatriptan) are first-line for moderate/severe attacks. NSAIDs (Naproxen, Ibuprofen) for milder attacks. Prophylaxis (if >=4 headache days/month): Beta-blockers (Propranolol), Anticonvulsants (Topiramate, Divalproex), or TCAs (Amitriptyline).
data.alternatives: CGRP receptor antagonists (Ubrogepant for acute; Erenumab for prevention), Lasmiditan (if triptans contraindicated), and OnabotulinumtoxinA (for chronic migraine). Neuromodulation devices (e-TNS, sTMS).
data.redFlags: SNOOP symptoms: Systemic signs (fever, weight loss), Neurological deficits, Onset sudden ('thunderclap'), Older age of onset (>50), Pattern change (first or worst headache). Rule out secondary causes like stroke, tumor, or meningitis. Avoid medication overuse headache (>10-15 days/month of acute meds).
data.followUp: Maintain a headache diary to track triggers and response. Follow-up 2-4 weeks after starting a new preventive regimen to check for side effects, then every 3-6 months. Goal is 50% reduction in attack frequency and improved quality of life.
